does: This paper empirically evaluates the performance cost of jailbreaking across different Claude model variants. The authors apply 28 distinct jailbreak techniques to Claude Haiku 4.5, Sonnet, and Opus 4.6 models, measuring both task performance degradation and jailbreak success rates on five benchmarks. They find that more capable models experience substantially smaller performance penalties when jailbroken: Haiku 4.5 shows an average 33.1% performance drop while Opus 4.6 with extended reasoning suffers only 7.7% degradation. The paper also identifies a key distinction between task types, showing reasoning-heavy tasks degrade more than knowledge retrieval tasks. Notably, they demonstrate that Boundary Point Jailbreaking achieves near-perfect evasion of safety classifiers with minimal capability loss across all tested models.
connects: This work directly extends prior research showing that complex jailbreaks often impose a performance cost or 'jailbreak tax' on target models. It builds on emerging understanding that safety mechanisms interact with model capabilities in subtle ways, building from earlier findings on safety-capability tradeoffs in alignment research. The paper relates to work on adversarial attacks and robustness testing of large language models, connecting to the broader literature examining whether safety training creates distinct 'safe' and 'unsafe' capabilities or whether jailbreaks simply activate existing latent knowledge. It responds to the assumption underlying prior safety cases that capability degradation provides a meaningful constraint on misuse, engaging with discourse around effective model governance and the limits of current safeguarding approaches.
enables: This research suggests that safety evaluation and deployment strategies for frontier models must move beyond assumptions that jailbreaks inherently reduce capability to use. It opens directions for investigating whether frontier model safety requires fundamentally different architectural or training approaches rather than relying on performance penalties as a protective mechanism. The findings point toward future work on capability-preserving safety mechanisms and suggest the need for safety cases based on behavioral alignment rather than capability constraints. The stark capability differences between model scales also enables research into how scaling affects the robustness of safety interventions, and whether certain safeguarding approaches may become ineffective at higher capability levels. This work implicitly calls for developing new evaluation frameworks and safety mechanisms tailored to frontier models where jailbreaks preserve functionality.
